title: State Transition Design

content.statement: Users evolve through stages; the product must guide transitions.
content.question: What are your business's user states (new, active, power, dormant), and is there a guided transition for each?
sop: Define your customer states (new, active, power user, dormant, churned) specifically for your business.
For your riskiest transition (e.g., new to active, or active to dormant), rate Clarity 1-10, does the customer know what to do next (see calculation, page 5).
Rate Relevance 1-10, is what you're offering them actually right for that stage (see calculation, page 5).
Rate Friction Reduction 1-10, how easy you've made that next step (see calculation, page 5).
Rate Drop-Off 1-10, how many customers currently fail that transition (see calculation, page 5), then build one specific nudge (email, offer, check-in) targeting your highest Drop-Off transition.
content.explainer: Users don't stay still. Design the transitions between states, not just the states themselves.
questions: What are your business's user states (new, active, power, dormant), and is there a guided transition for each?
When a customer goes quiet, does anything in your business actually notice and respond, or do they just fall off the list?
What's the one nudge that would move a first-time customer toward becoming a repeat one, and does it currently exist?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Clarity 7, Relevance 8, Friction Reduction 6. 7 times 8 times 6 equals 336, divided by Drop-Off 4 equals 84. Higher means customers move smoothly from one stage to the next. Lower means a specific transition is where you're quietly losing people.
content.closing_statement: Every stage a user leaves needs a guided exit.

caption:
Principle Nº 83: State Transition Design

Users evolve through stages; the product must guide transitions. Users don't stay still.

Ask yourself: What are your business's user states (new, active, power, dormant), and is there a guided transition for each?
